spec_match.rationale: Demonstrates cold-vs-warm fetch timing via the Cache API but never actually exercises the cross-site partitioned-vs-shared keying that the feature is about — it is a generic cache-hit demo that would have worked identically before Chrome 86.

interactive.rationale: Run demo / Clear cache buttons drive a real Cache API + Resource Timing measurement, animating bars and emitting a speedup ratio — past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The feature index blurb promises 'Cache API and fetch timing to illustrate cache hit versus miss behaviour'; the page does exactly that, even if it under-sells the actual partitioning story.

edge_cases.rationale: Only the happy hit/miss path is shown. No covering of the allowlist mismatch case, no demonstration that non-pervasive resources still partition, no Vary header / opaque response / range-request edge cases.

references.rationale: The see-also block links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5202380930678784 directly.

design_system.rationale: All colours come from CSS variables, buttons inherit the global black-on-ivory rule with no overrides, no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Stage a real cross-site comparison, not a single-site cache hit
openQuestions.detail: The whole feature is that the cache key drops the top-level site for allowlisted URLs. A single-site cache-hit demo cannot show this. Add an iframe or simulated 'second top-level site' panel that fetches the same allowlisted URL and shows it hitting the shared bucket vs. missing in a partitioned world. Without that the demo doesn't actually demonstrate the feature.

openQuestions.title: Contrast allowlisted vs. non-allowlisted fetches
openQuestions.detail: Run the demo twice — once with an allowlist member (jQuery from ajax.googleapis.com) and once with a self-hosted lookalike — to show that only the former benefits. Right now both code paths would behave identically because the underlying single-site cache works for everything.

openQuestions.title: Surface transferSize / encodedBodySize / decodedBodySize for memory vs disk cache hits
openQuestions.detail: The code-card mentions 'memory cache (sub-millisecond) vs disk cache (1–5ms)' but the live readout doesn't actually distinguish them. Pull entry.deliveryType once Chrome ships it, or fall back to inferring from durations and surface the cache tier in the timing card.

openQuestions.title: Show what happens after a Cache-Control purge
openQuestions.detail: If a pervasive resource ages out or is removed from the allowlist mid-session, what does the developer see? Add a 'simulate evict' button that clears the entry and re-runs the second fetch as a cold network call.

summary: Honest live Cache-API + Resource-Timing demo but it doesn't actually demonstrate the cross-site sharing the feature is about — you would get the same hit/miss curves before Chrome 146. Pair it with an iframe-based cross-site comparison and a non-allowlisted fetch to make the partitioning story visible.
